statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given two numbers $ n $ and $ m $ .

An undirected graph is called beautiful if it satisfies the following conditions:

- It has no loops or multiple edges.
- It has exactly $ n $ vertices and $ m $ edges.
- Its vertices cannot be divided into $ 2 $ sets such that the sums of the degrees of all vertices in the first set and the sums of the degrees of all vertices in the second set are equal.

You need to either report that a beautiful undirected graph with the given $ n, m $ does not exist, or provide one.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The only line of each test case contains two integers $ n, m $ ( $ 12 \leq n \leq 10^5, 1 \leq m \leq \min(2 \cdot 10^5, \frac{n(n-1)}{2}) $ ) — the number of vertices and edges in the graph.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 10^5 $ , and the sum of $ m $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 2 \cdot 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output "No" if such a graph does not exist. Otherwise, output "Yes", and then, in the following $ m $ lines — the edges of the beautiful graph in arbitrary order. If there are multiple answers, you may output any of them.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
12 7
12 1
90000 12
30 434
30 435
Output
Yes
1 2
2 3
3 4
4 5
5 1
1 3
2 4
No
Yes
1 4
1 5
1 6
2 4
2 5
2 6
3 4
3 5
3 6
4 5
4 6
5 6
No
No

Note
In the first test case, the graph is a simple cycle of $ 7 $ vertices. The degrees of the vertices are $ \{2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\} $ . It is easy to see that in such a graph, it is impossible to divide the vertices into $ 2 $ parts with equal sums of degrees.

In the second test case, since $ m = 1 $ , it is easy to see that the vertices can always be divided into $ 2 $ parts with equal sums of degrees. Therefore, no solution exists.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2196F - Indivisible
First solution
:
Let's start by solving the problem for odd $$$n$$$. Intuitively, if for an odd $$$n$$$ we can make all vertex degrees almost equal and as large as possible, there should be a good chance that the set of degrees will be indivisible.
We divide the sum of all degrees, $$$2m$$$, by $$$n$$$ with a remainder. Let $$$2m = q \cdot n + r$$$. $$$0 \le r  \lt  n$$$.
We can make the vertex degrees almost equal if they are $$$q, \ldots, q, q+1, \ldots, q+1$$$, where $$$q+1$$$ appears $$$r$$$ times, and $$$q$$$ appears $$$n-r$$$ times. A graph with such degrees can always be constructed; let's consider two cases:
$$$q$$$ and $$$r$$$ are even numbers. Mentally arrange the vertices in a circle from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$, where $$$n$$$ and $$$1$$$ will also be neighboring vertices. Connect vertices $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$ with an edge if the minimum distance between them
around the circle
does not exceed $$$\frac{q}{2}$$$. After this, each vertex will have a degree equal to $$$q$$$. Then we add $$$\frac{r}{2}$$$ edges between pairs of vertices $$$1 \lt = \gt \frac{n}{2}+1, \ldots, r \lt = \gt \frac{n}{2}+r$$$.
$$$q$$$ and $$$r$$$ are odd numbers. Since $$$n$$$ is odd and $$$q \lt n$$$, it is also true that $$$q+1  \lt  n$$$, which means we can construct a configuration from the first case where each vertex degree is $$$q+1$$$. After that, we remove edges $$$1 \lt = \gt 2, \ldots, n-r-1 \lt = \gt n-r$$$.
There are no other cases since $$$n$$$ is odd, and $$$2m = q \cdot n + r$$$, which means modulo two, $$$q + r$$$ equals $$$0$$$.
Let's analyze in which cases this graph will meet the problem's condition. For the condition to be violated, there must be a solution to the equation $$$qa + (q+1)b = m$$$, where $$$0 \leq a \leq n-r, 0 \leq b \leq r$$$. Additionally, we fix the condition $$$a+b  \lt  \frac{n}{2}$$$; this must be satisfied for one of the halves of the partition (since the total number of vertices is $$$n$$$, the smaller half has fewer than $$$\frac{n}{2}$$$ vertices when $$$n$$$ is odd). We transform the equation:
$$$$$$qa + (q+1)b = m$$$$$$
$$$$$$q(a+b) + b = m$$$$$$
$$$$$$2q(a+b) + 2b = 2m$$$$$$
$$$$$$2q(a+b) + 2b = qn + r$$$$$$
$$$$$$q(2a+2b-n) = r - 2b$$$$$$
$$$$$$q(n - 2a - 2b) = 2b - r$$$$$$
Let's take a closer look at the rewritten equation. The left side is guaranteed to be an integer $$$\geq q$$$, since $$$n  \gt  2a+2b$$$.
We will show that when $$$r \lt q$$$, the equation has no solutions. When $$$r \lt q$$$, since $$$b \le r$$$, we get that $$$2b - r \le r$$$, meaning the right side of the equation $$$\le r$$$, while the left side equals $$$q$$$, and in the case $$$r  \lt  q$$$, such an equation is unsolvable. Thus, if $$$r  \lt  q$$$, we have shown that the graph
satisfies
the problem's condition.
Let's continue the chain of inequalities, each time
weakening
the inequality.
$$$$$$(q + 1) \cdot (n - 2a - 2b) = n - 2a - r$$$$$$
$$$$$$q + 1 \le n - 2a - r$$$$$$
$$$$$$q + 1 + 2a + r \le n$$$$$$
$$$$$$q + r  \lt  n$$$$$$
It follows that from the assumption that a solution to the equation exists, we derive the fact that $$$q + r  \lt  n$$$. From this, it follows that when $$$n \le q + r$$$, there is no solution to the equation, and the graph
satisfies
the problem's condition.
Let's consider the following strategy: while $$$\frac{(n-2)(n-3)}{2} \geq m$$$, we decrease $$$n$$$ by $$$2$$$ and reduce the problem to $$$(n-2, m)$$$, isolating two vertices of degree $$$0$$$. In the end, we will obtain a problem where $$$n(n-1) \ge 2m$$$ and $$$(n-2)(n-3) \lt 2m$$$, while $$$n$$$ is still odd.
We will show that in this case (when $$$n(n-1) \ge 2m$$$ and $$$(n-2)(n-3) \lt 2m$$$) one of the two conditions ($$$n \le q+r$$$ or $$$r  \lt  q$$$) will always be satisfied, except for the case when $$$m \in \{1, 2, 4, 6, 12\}$$$.
$$$$$$n(n-1) \geq 2m  \gt  n^2-5n+6$$$$$$
$$$$$$n-1 \geq \lfloor \frac{2m}{n} \rfloor  \gt  n-5+\frac{6}{n}-1$$$$$$
$$$$$$n-1 \geq q  \gt  n-6+\frac{6}{n}$$$$$$
Let's analyze the cases.
$$$q = n-1$$$
In this case, the graph must be complete, and for it $$$r=0$$$, thus the condition $$$r  \lt  q$$$ is satisfied.
$$$q = n-2$$$
If $$$r  \lt  n-2$$$, then $$$r  \lt  q$$$.
If $$$2 \le r$$$, then $$$n \le q + r$$$.
The extreme case where both conditions are not satisfied is $$$n=3$$$, then $$$m=2$$$.
$$$q = n-3$$$
If $$$r  \lt  n-3$$$, then $$$r  \lt  q$$$.
If $$$3 \le r$$$, then $$$n \le q + r$$$.
The extreme cases where both conditions are not satisfied are $$$n=3$$$, $$$m=1$$$ and $$$n = 5$$$, $$$m = 6$$$.
$$$q = n-4$$$
If $$$r  \lt  n-4$$$, then $$$r  \lt  q$$$.
If $$$4 \le r$$$, then $$$n \le q + r$$$.
The extreme cases where both conditions are not satisfied are $$$n = 7$$$, $$$m = 12$$$ and $$$n = 5$$$, $$$m = 4$$$.
$$$q = n-5$$$
The unweakened condition: $$$2m  \gt  n^2-5n+6$$$, thus $$$2m - qn  \gt  6$$$, meaning $$$r  \gt  6$$$. Therefore, the condition $$$n \le q+r$$$ in this case is always satisfied.
Thus, our strategy has been proven to yield an indivisible graph for odd $$$n$$$, and $$$m \notin \{1,2,4,6,12\}$$$!
By bruteforce, it is easy to show that all possible graphs with $$$m \in \{1,2,4,6\}$$$ will be divisible. For example, for $$$m=2$$$, the possible vertex degrees are either $$$\{1,1,1,1\}$$$ or $$$\{1,1,2\}$$$.
And for $$$m=12$$$, it is possible to create a graph on $$$6$$$ vertices (and for all $$$n  \gt  6$$$, we can take this graph and $$$n-6$$$ isolated vertices) that satisfies the indivisibility condition, with degrees $$$\{3,3,3,5,5,5\}$$$, which is $$$K_6$$$ without three edges between the first three vertices.
Thus, the solution for odd $$$n$$$ is
No
for $$$m \in \{1,2,4,6\}$$$, with the case $$$m=12$$$ handled separately; otherwise, the strategy works: take the densest graph with $$$m$$$ edges and $$$\le n$$$ vertices, supplemented with isolated vertices.
Now let's solve the problem for even $$$n$$$. Immediately, we can discard a cluster of cases using the solution for odd:
If $$$m \in \{1,2,4,6\}$$$, the answer is still no.
If $$$m = 12$$$, the same example will work.
If $$$m \le \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}$$$, we can isolate one vertex and reduce the problem to $$$(n-1, m)$$$, and $$$n-1$$$ will be odd, thus we know that with such parameters a solution always exists.
The only remaining case: $$$2m  \gt  (n-1)(n-2)$$$.
Notice that in this case, if the graph has a partition of vertices into two sets with equal sum of degrees, then both of these sets must have size $$$\frac{n}{2}$$$, otherwise, the smaller set has size $$$\le \frac{n}{2}-1$$$, and thus the maximum sum of degrees of this set: $$$(n-1)(\frac{n}{2}-1) = (n-1)\frac{n-2}{2}$$$, while $$$m$$$ is strictly greater than this number.
Knowing this, we note that in this case, the condition of indivisibility for this graph is equivalent to the condition of indivisibility for the reverse graph, but with the added condition that the vertex sets must have equal size.
In the reverse graph, there will be $$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2} - m  \lt  n - 1$$$ edges. If $$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2} -m$$$ is not equal to $$$0, 1, 2, 4, 6$$$, we can take an indivisible reverse graph on $$$n-1$$$ vertices and $$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2} - m$$$ edges, using the construction above. Since this graph is indivisible in general form, it is also indivisible under the added condition of equal set sizes.
It remains to deal with the cases when $$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2} - m \in \{0,1,2,4,6\}$$$. Here we can recall the restriction $$$n \ge 12$$$. It turns out that for $$$n \ge 12$$$, any graph with $$$0,1,2,4,6$$$ edges is not just always divisible, but always divisible into two sets of equal size. This is true because in a regular partition, the sizes of both sets do not exceed $$$6$$$ (if we only take non-isolated vertices), thus we can take a regular partition and supplement it with isolated vertices so that both sets have size $$$6$$$. And for $$$n  \gt  12$$$, we can take a pair of isolated vertices in different sets and reduce the problem to $$$n - 2$$$. Thus, in this case, there is no solution to the problem.
Second solution
(thanks to [user:SomethingNew] for the alternative solution!):
Let's note/guess/suppose that the graph answer cannot have
more than 3
different degrees. More precisely, it is sufficient to consider only graphs with no more than 3 different degrees to cover all possible $$$(n,m)$$$ where a solution exists.
Let's bruteforce all possible sets of degrees and use the Erdős–Gallai theorem to check whether a graph can be formed from these degrees, in $$$O(n \log n)$$$. Checking the indivisibility criterion can also be done quite quickly, given that there are no more than $$$3$$$ different degrees (they can be arranged in $$$O(\log n)$$$ knapsack items), and using a bitset.
Such a solution already works, but is obviously too slow. However, we can cleverly "tweak" the parameters of the enumeration, trying to guess the properties of the construction so that it works in terms of time.
Enumerate the actual $$$n$$$ (without isolated vertices) in increasing order, starting from the first such that $$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \ge m$$$.
Enumerate the maximum degree value only from $$$n$$$ to $$$n-5$$$.
Honestly enumerate the number of vertices with the maximum degree, as well as the minimum degree, after which we honestly enumerate the average degree. Of course, add as many
break
as possible.
It is claimed that such bruteforce will run in $$$O(m \sqrt m \log m)$$$ time, as all considered values of $$$n$$$ will be $$$O(\sqrt m)$$$
